reasoning: Neither `container_perms_wf` nor `container_tree_wf` constrains `owned_threads` at all. The preconditions are simultaneously satisfiable, and the ensures confirms two distinct containers can own the same thread, violating the unique ownership model described in the code comments.

The ensures merely restates a precondition, but the real finding is that `new_container_ensures` never requires `new_container_perms[new_container_ptr].value().owned_threads@ =~= Set::empty()`. A freshly created container should start with no owned threads, yet the spec permits arbitrary thread sets.

All seven `new_container_preserve_tree_inv_*` lemmas are `external_body`, meaning the entire proof that container creation preserves `container_tree_wf` is axiomatically assumed. While the postconditions are likely correct given the detailed `new_container_ensures` preconditions, this is a real soundness gap — any bug in the specification of `new_container_ensures` would go undetected.

The spec never relates `scheduler` contents to `owned_threads`. A container can schedule threads it doesn't own, breaking the containment model where only owned threads should be schedulable by a container.

Neither `container_perms_wf` nor `container_tree_wf` constrains `owned_procs` beyond requiring `children.wf()` and `children.unique()`. The same process can appear in two distinct containers' `owned_procs` lists, violating the single-parent ownership model for processes.
